topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The acceptance letter felt like a brittle, misplaced piece of paper in my hand. I reread the fine print, my eyes skipping over the lines detailing my tuition waiver, until a section caught my attention—a name that wasn't mine. A sudden, cold prickle traced its way down my spine. I looked up from the document, the fluorescent lights of the library buzzing overhead seeming to intensify. My hands started to tremble, and I had to grip the edge of the table to keep from dropping the paper. Who was this person? The realization hit me not as a sudden jolt, but as a slow, heavy sinking feeling in my chest, leaving me unable to draw a full breath. I pushed the chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafening in the quiet study hall. Everything seemed tilted, the familiar architecture of my future suddenly unstable.

[story 2]

I stared at the official notification on my screen, the words blurring into an indecipherable mess. The financial aid package I’d worked so hard for—the one that meant everything—was addressed to another applicant. My fingers hovered over the mouse, refusing to click, as if the screen itself were a foreign object. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, making the room swim slightly. I leaned back in my worn desk chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath me. I tried to focus on the textbook open beside me, but the ink seemed to bleed together, refusing to form coherent sentences. A hollow echo resonated in my ears, a sound that suggested something fundamental had shifted, leaving me adrift in a sea of unanswered questions.

[story 3]

The envelope was sealed with official wax, the seal unbroken, yet the contents felt utterly alien. I traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, the texture of the paper rough beneath my skin. Every sentence about the scholarship, the dedication, the future opportunities—it all pointed away from me. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my stomach clench painfully. I stood up abruptly, knocking my water bottle slightly askew. I walked to the window, staring out at the campus quad, but the vibrant green scenery seemed muted, distant. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. It was as if the ground beneath my feet had dissolved, leaving me standing on shifting sand, unsure of which way was up or forward.
